metadata.format_notes: User messages are verbatim. Assistant responses are substantive summaries (actual responses were often long; full decisions captured in PROPOSAL.md and proposal-critique-v02-revision.md). Tool-use descriptions omitted; only conversational exchange captured.

turns.user: Read all files under /Users/cwoolley/workspace/livespec/brainstorming/approach-2-nlspec-based. Critique the PROPOSAL.md and associated files on completeness to be ready to seed a bootstrapped SPECIFICATION for this repo. Use the same process as described for the critique skill in the proposal.
turns.assistant: Read all files in brainstorming/approach-2-nlspec-based (PROPOSAL.md, livespec-nlspec-spec.md, nlspec-spec.md, goals-and-non-goals.md, subdomains-and-unsolved-routing.md, 3 lifecycle diagram files, terminology summary, prior-art.md, and v001/v002 history). Produced 'Proposal Critique v02 - Completeness for Bootstrapping a SPECIFICATION' structured per v001 critique pattern: Purpose, What Is Strong, 8 Major Gaps blocking seeding (M1-M8), 10 Significant Gaps (S9-S18), 13 Cleanup items (C19-C31), Application of Recreatability Test, Recommended Path to Seedable Spec, Notes on Material Outside PROPOSAL.md. Findings labeled by failure mode: ambiguity, malformation, incompleteness, incorrectness.

turns.user: Write this critique to a file for safekeeping, then go through every item and interview me to answer the questions, then update it based on my responses.
turns.assistant: Created proposed_changes/v003-proposed-change-proposal-critique-v02.md with the full critique. Created 19 tracking tasks (one per major/significant item plus one for batched cleanup). Started M1 interview with 4 batched sub-questions (M1a packaging, M1b dispatch, M1c prompting, M1d bundle location).

turns.user_via_rejection: Gave tentative answers: M1a=combo of options 2 and 3 (subfile split + shell scripts, @ commands for deterministic reads); M1b=2 (shell parser + routing); M1c=both (LLM prompt with fallback); M1d=no answer. Asked for clarification.
turns.assistant: Asked what they wanted to clarify, noting tentative answers captured.

turns.user:
1. NEW REQUIREMENT: This project will be formed as a plugin project to install the skill. Start with standard claude plugin structure `.claude-plugin/skills/livespec/...`. Future V2 support for opencode and pi (https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono/tree/main/packages/coding-agent) plugins.

2. The .claude-plugins/skills/livespec skill directory will be symlinked to the repos `.claude/skills/livespec` directory, so that the live version is usable within the repo.

3. plugin.json is defined and filled, but any publishing to anthropic marketplace/etc is deferred to v2
turns.assistant: Captured full M1 resolution: plugin delivery model (new scope), subfile skill split with shared helpers, shell parser routing, LLM-mediated prompts with structured-tool fallback, symlink from .claude-plugin/skills/livespec/ to .claude/skills/livespec/, plugin.json defined. Marketplace + opencode + pi-mono to v2. Marked M1 complete, moved to M2 with batched sub-questions (M2a language, M2b output contract, M2c check #9 handoff).

turns.user_via_rejection: For M2a: will create brainstorming/approach-2-nlspec-based/bash-skill-script-style-requirements.md as companion for SPECIFICATION/constraints.md, including shellcheck + static-analysis enforced via git hooks/CI. Other M2 questions unanswered.
turns.assistant: Asked what to clarify; confirmed M2a=Bash with upcoming companion requirements doc.

turns.user: Is there any reason to have human-readable text? Where would a human ever see this if it is invoked via a skill?
turns.assistant: Agreed - no human audience in skill flow (Bash tool output goes to LLM, which narrates). Locked M2b=JSON on stdout. Re-asked M2c (check #9 handoff) and M2d (script location) batched.

turns.user_via_rejection: Wanted clarification; neither M2c nor M2d answered.
turns.assistant: Asked what to clarify.

turns.user: You have not printed the sub-items for M2 anywhere that I can see them. You have to print them before I can discuss them with you.
turns.assistant: Apologized. Printed M2a/M2b/M2c/M2d sub-items clearly with options and status. Asked M2c and M2d together.

turns.user: Why are you asking me two things at once? Ask me one thing at a time.
turns.assistant: Acknowledged. Saved feedback memory (feedback_one_question_at_a_time.md). Proceeded with M2c alone, describing the three options for check #9 cross-phase handoff.

turns.user:
Aren't we fundamentally thinking of this wrong. If there is drift here, then there is no choice but to backfill the missing Version. It is not a standard proposed and revised cycle that the user has to be involved in. We just have to backfill the missing version unconditionally. Therefore 1. We should just always create a proposal with a diff explaining that it was due to a drift detected by Running Doctor, 2. create an acknowledgment that acknowledges it , 3. Copy the current content to the backfilled missing version. 4. Abort the doctor script and prompt the user to commit the changes, then rerun doctor after it has been committed.

This also implies if this check should be skipped with a message if there are any uncommitted changes to the actual specification files (as defined by the template), because otherwise there will by definition be drift because changes are in progress. I think any other alternative would be too complex to implement, with too many edge cases. Push back if you disagree.
turns.assistant: Agreed unconditional backfill is cleaner than interactive prompt. Pushed back on uncommitted-changes skip: requires reading git state which is v1 non-goal. Offered three git-dependency options: tighten non-goal (allow read-only), drop the skip, or condition on .git presence. Also flagged non-git projects handling and proposed 'livespec-doctor' as author identifier.

turns.user: We can't always fire. That would write a bogus change and version if the user is directly editing the file, right?
turns.assistant: Conceded - retracted the 'drop the skip' option. Accepted narrow read-only git dependency with tightened non-goal wording: livespec MUST NOT write to git, MAY read git state. Asked about non-git project behavior with three options.
Recorded M2c: auto-backfill on drift; skip in WIP (git dirty); skip entirely in non-git projects; livespec-doctor as fixed author identifier. Moved to M2d (script location).
Recorded M2d: .claude-plugin/skills/livespec/scripts/doctor-static.sh. M2 complete. Moved to M3 (revise modification mechanism), asking M3a about who authors the modification when decision is modify.

turns.user: Are you talking about per proposal file or per individual item in a single proposal file? Is there any existing docs about this, or are you figuring this out from scratch now?
turns.assistant: Clarified: per-file per current PROPOSAL.md (one decision per file even though a file can bundle multiple changes). Existing docs: almost nothing (only the acknowledgement format mentions ## Modifications). I was figuring from scratch. Flagged lurking design question about per-item decisions. Re-asked M3a.

turns.user: Okay, the revise section is currently wrong. We should absolutely support multiple proposals in a single file, and the revise will have to make a decision on each one.
turns.assistant: Acknowledged significant structural change. Asked which multi-proposal file shape to adopt: flat ## Proposal: <name> sections, per-proposal YAML blocks, or keep existing + add items-list inside Proposed Changes (backwards-compatible).

turns.user: What is a target partition?
turns.assistant: Explained: field from current YAML front-matter listing which specification files (spec.md, contracts.md, etc.) the proposal touches. Purpose: doctor sanity-check + revise partition knowledge. In multi-proposal shape, target_partitions would move per-proposal.

turns.user:
Just forget about the previous V002. When you're brainstorming, you don't have to obsess over the history here.

do 1 - flat sections.

And the user should be prompted to accept or modify each modification during the revise, but with additional option to skip further confirmation for that proposal file and have LLM automatically address (accept/modify/reject) all remaining proposals in the file
turns.assistant: Saved feedback memory (feedback_brainstorming_no_history_obsession.md) about not preserving backward compatibility during brainstorming. Captured decisions: flat ## Proposal: sections with sub-headings, file-level front-matter for topic/author/created_at, per-proposal target_partitions, per-proposal accept/modify/reject with delegate-remaining-to-LLM toggle. Asked M3a again: dialog vs user-authored vs LLM-authored.

turns.user: 3, unless user has skipped all confirmations for the proposal file.
turns.assistant: Recorded M3a: dialogue mode default; LLM auto-commits its own modification when file delegated. Asked M3b about proposal file handling when moved to history.

turns.user: 1. But I think we need to change the domain language here, from `acknowledgement` to `revision`. this captures the meaning more, and also matches the command name that generates it. Any reason not to do that? If we do, ensure it's updated everywhere.
turns.assistant: Recorded M3b=1 (preserve byte-identical; delta in ## Modifications). Pushed back on 'revision' rename: 'revision' is already used throughout as general noun/verb for act of revising. Making it also a file type overloads the term. Offered alternatives: decision, disposition, keep acknowledgement.

turns.user: I don't understand why you consider this overloading. All three things you listed are part of the same cohesive action, correct? Push back if I'm wrong.
turns.assistant: Conceded - 'commit' in git denotes act, record, and state without being overloaded. Same scaling works for revision. Recorded terminology change: acknowledgement -> revision everywhere. Asked M3c about ## Modifications format.

turns.user: Isn't the unified diff format just asking for conflicts as the file is modified throughout the revision process?
turns.assistant: Conceded - unified diffs need line-number anchors, but within a multi-proposal revise earlier proposals shift anchors for later ones. Line numbers stale instantly. Locked M3c: prose description for ## Modifications, optional short before/after excerpts for hyper-local clarity. Moved to M3d about freeform-only revise.

turns.user: 1. Push back on 'free form text' as the best name for this parameter and concept.
turns.assistant: Agreed - the domain term is 'intent' per the NLSpec guidelines. All three uses (seed, propose-change, revise) are instances of intent. Renamed <freeform text> -> <intent>. Recorded M3d: auto-create ephemeral vNNN-proposed-change-intent-<timestamp>.md.

turns.user: No, we're not done with them. Revise is a different case because that is not the only avenue for intent to come in. There's also the files.
turns.assistant: Asked clarification: is the distinction that <intent> works for seed and propose-change (sole avenue) but revise needs a supplementary name because intent also comes from proposed_changes/ files? Offered <additional-intent>, <inline-intent>, <ad-hoc-intent>.

turns.user: additional-intent is perfect. continue
turns.assistant: Recorded: seed <intent>, propose-change <topic> <intent>, revise <additional-intent>. Moved to M4.

turns.user: So you are naming the auto-created proposal file *-additional-intent-*, right?
turns.assistant: Yes - updated M3d to vNNN-proposed-change-additional-intent-<timestamp>.md. Re-asked M4 objection check.

turns.user: no
turns.assistant: Recorded M4: delete 'per the progressive-disclosure mapping below' dangling clause. Moved to M5 (critique->propose-change output contract).
Recorded M5a: critique prompt emits raw findings, skill reshapes into proposed-change file. Asked M5b about finding-to-proposal mapping.
Recorded M5b: one finding = one proposal. Asked M5c about where per-proposal fields come from.

turns.user: What is the best choice considering that we want to make the critique prompt templatizable? We don't want to place too many constraints on users for how they want to define the critique prompt. Or do we? Should we require it to emit JSON, structured JSON?
turns.assistant: Recommended structured JSON with documented schema: trivial skill implementation, precise contract for prompt authors (structural not semantic constraint), LLMs reliably follow JSON schemas, skill can validate and re-prompt. Proposed findings schema with id/name/target_partitions/summary/motivation/proposed_change fields.

turns.user: Yes, that works, but does that change our prior decision? About how the critique rate relates to the proposed change skill
turns.assistant: Refined not overridden. M5a still holds (skill reshapes - just mechanical now). M5b still holds (one finding=one proposal). M5c picks option 1 via JSON format. Locked flow: critique prompt -> JSON findings array -> skill parses/validates -> wraps each as ## Proposal -> adds front-matter -> writes file.

turns.user: Continue.
turns.assistant: M5 complete. Moved to M6 (openspec silent-failure path) with three options: treat like custom template failure, keep reservation with consistent error, or drop entirely.
Recorded M6: drop openspec from v1 entirely. Only livespec is valid template value. Openspec becomes v2 material. Moved to M7a (seed intent dereferencing).

turns.user: I don't understand your question. Isn't this going to get passed directly to the LLM, and it will handle the natural language and find any files that are referenced automatically? Why are you even asking this question? What am I missing?
turns.assistant: Conceded - was thinking like a CLI, but this runs in an LLM context. LLM handles references naturally. Dropped M7a. Moved to M7b about heading-extraction nondeterminism.

turns.user: Isn't the spec template going to determine this? I think you are missing a deeper question of what is the format of the spec template and how it is processed when providing new intent via seed or revise.
turns.assistant: Agreed. Paused M7 to surface template format question. Offered 4 options for what a template IS: skeleton files with placeholders, skeleton + template.json metadata, prompts + skeleton files (Option 3 - most flexible), or trivial 'starting content' with skill holding all smarts.

turns.user: 3 clearly seems the best for a robust and extensible system that lets people have control over how it works while still enforcing the overall process. Do you agree, or do you have any pushback?
turns.assistant: Agreed Option 3 is right long-term. Flagged three consequences: (1) custom templates effectively in v1 scope (can't deny access to the mechanism), (2) prompts become part of spec surface requiring contracts specification, (3) template format versioning needed. Recommended accept Option 3 with tightenings.

turns.user:
Yes, we are going with option three. On your comments:

1. Yes, it is in scope.

2. Is there any problem with that? The config file already refers to the template, and that's part of the "Spec surface" too. Push back if I'm missing anything.

3. Okay, the versioning. But I don't think it needs to be in the template's metadata; it should be in the live spec JSON config file next to the template type. Otherwise, the doctor has to worry about iterating over a bunch of files and checking their individual versions and having a bunch more logic. Correct?

One other thing to change is that we no longer have a stand-alone critique prompt entry in the config file. It will just be part of the template like the other command prompts.

And you need to have some clear guidance about how this is composed and make the architectural boundary clear between the prompts that are part of the skill and the prompts that are part of the template and how they are invoked.
turns.assistant: Addressed each point. (1) Confirmed custom templates v1 in-scope, doctor check #13 reshaped. (2) Agreed prompts on spec surface is fine. (3) Pushed back on version placement: Option A (template.json) is a single metadata file, not 'many files', same complexity as reading .livespec.jsonc. Config-based version becomes unilateral claim that can lie; breaks single-source-of-truth. Recommended template.json with optional minimum-version belt-and-suspenders in config. (4) Confirmed custom_critique_prompt removal. (5) Articulated architectural boundary: skill owns lifecycle+invariants, template owns content generation; skill invokes template prompts with fixed input/output variables; skill doesn't decide content placement; template doesn't write files/manage versions/prompt user.

turns.user: Okay, I understand now the template has to declare its version in template.json, and the live spec config declares which template version it is currently expecting to use.
turns.assistant: Recorded both-levels versioning. Articulated full architectural boundary with lifecycle/invariants on skill side and content generation on template side. Described invocation contract (skill->template prompt with variables, validated output, retry on malformed, abort after N). Asked whether boundary matched intent.

turns.user:
1. You need to explain more what you are expecting in skeletons. And how do we distinguish between templates for the spec itself and templates for the commands? Or is the spec template itself just associated with the seed command? How do you know what skeleton goes with which command?

2. Everything in this communication layer should be JSON, with schemas defined and enforced. Side question: Can we even do JSON schema enforcement in Bash?
turns.assistant: On skeletons: per-partition not per-command; consumed primarily by seed; one template bundles both skeleton files and command prompts; proposed directory structure. Auto-generated READMEs in proposed_changes/ and history/ move to skill level. On JSON+schemas: confirmed. Discussed JSON schema enforcement options in Bash: jq (lightweight, partial), ajv-cli (npm full validator), Go/Rust single-binary, LLM self-validation. Incorrectly recommended ajv-cli assuming Claude Code runs on Node.

turns.user:
2. Okay, make JQ a requirement. If it is not available on the system, then the skill should abort and prompt the user to install it with an OS-appropriate suggestion. This also means that we can allow all of the LLMs to rely on it as well. Instead of having to do manual parsing, they should still do JSON schema compliance as well wherever possible, come out with hard-coded schemas.

One clarification on the template directory structure. instead of `specification` subdir, name it `specification-template`, and content is relative to the ROOT of the repo. So, for default, it would be `specification-template/SPECIFICATION/spec.md`, etc.... This also allows flexibility for spec files to live directly in the root of the repo, correct?
turns.assistant: Corrected: Claude Code is native, not Node. Accepted jq as hard runtime dep with abort+install suggestion. Bash limited to file-level checks without jq; structural JSON goes to LLM phase. Retracted 'skeletons' terminology (contains actual starter content, not skeleton). Retracted 'partitions' terminology, replaced with 'specification file' / 'specification-file' in JSON keys. Agreed specification-template/ content relative to repo root, enabling templates to place files anywhere. Confirmed consequences: .livespec.jsonc specification_dir becomes moot (drop), doctor check #3 derives files from template dir. Asked clarification: are prompts/ and specification-template/ paths configurable in template.json or fixed-by-convention.

turns.user: A, B, and C are confirmed. On D, no, they're not configurable. They must be fixed by convention. Template JSON only declares the version.
turns.assistant: Recorded: template.json contains only {template_format_version: 1}; prompts/ and specification-template/ are fixed-by-convention. M7 now dissolved - template architecture resolves heading-extraction concerns. Moved to M8 (.livespec.jsonc defaults format).
Recorded M8: fully commented .livespec.jsonc on first seed. Moved to S9 (BCP 14 check #10 decidability).

turns.user: This seems very brittle and error-prone. Suggestions?
turns.assistant: Reworked. Three narrow cases actually worth catching deterministically: (1) misspelled uppercase BCP 14 keywords (finite list, dictionary lookup), (2) mixed-case BCP 14 keywords (almost always mistakes), (3) lowercase BCP 14 token within a sentence also containing uppercase BCP 14 (inconsistency signal). Everything else leave alone; semantic questions move to LLM phase.

turns.user: What do you mean, "flag for review"? What is the exact doc skill behavior you are talking about?
turns.assistant: Conceded 'flag' was fuzzy. Doctor's static phase is pass-or-fail only. Reformulated split: static phase hard-fails on cases 1+2 (misspelled uppercase, mixed-case standalone); LLM-driven phase surfaces case 3 (sentence-level mixed usage) + general BCP 14 conformance as findings that user can accept/defer/dismiss.

turns.user: ok good
turns.assistant: S9 resolved. Moved to S10 (Gherkin check #11 false-positives).

turns.user:
Option one is good.

But we also must enforce that each individual line within has a blank line between them, otherwise Markdown will run them all together as a single paragraph.

And we already said we don't want to use fenced code blocks for this. Monospaced is too hard to read, and then you can't use Markdown formatting in it.

Sound good?
turns.assistant: Recorded S10 full rule: scope restricted to inside scenario blocks (Scenario: line to next Scenario: or EOF); within such blocks, every Gherkin keyword line preceded+followed by blank line; fenced code blocks containing Gherkin MUST NOT appear; prose outside scenario blocks unaffected. Moved to S11.

Recorded S11: revision front-matter has both reviser_llm (host metadata with unknown-llm fallback) and reviser_human (git user.name/email with unknown fallback). Moved to S12 (cross-file reference boundaries).
Recorded S12a (rule applies to README.md linking spec files). Moved to S12b.
Recorded S12b (rule applies to proposed-change files). Moved to S12c.

turns.user: Clarify exactly why you are asking all of this? What enforces it? Are we talking about doctor?
turns.assistant: Clarified two layers: format rule (prescriptive) and doctor check #12 (enforcement - scans links, verifies file exists and anchor resolves). Distinguished rule scope, doctor scan scope, doctor resolution scope. Reframed as single question with 4 scope options: tight (spec files only), medium (+ README + proposed-change), broad (all markdown links), or broad-rule/medium-enforcement.
Recorded S12: broad scope. Rule and doctor #12 cover all markdown links in all SPECIFICATION/ files. Moved to S13.

Recorded S13: SPECIFICATION/README.md generated from template starter at seed, then fully editable as normal spec file. Moved to S14 (versioning width transition) with proposed rule: numeric parsing, max-width zero-padding, historic widths immutable.

turns.user: Except
turns.assistant: Message cut off; asked for completion.

turns.user: accept
turns.assistant: S14 accepted. Moved to S15 (propose-change intent-to-proposal shape).

turns.user: Any reason not to pick 2? Does this work when it's invoked programmatically, for example, via critique? And does it work if you just want to use the intent to point it to an existing file?
turns.assistant: Explained yes to both with short-circuit: propose-change accepts two input shapes. Structured findings JSON (from critique) skips LLM reshape, skill wraps directly. Raw <intent> text invokes template's prompt for LLM reshape. File references work naturally via LLM @-handling. Recommended option 2 with JSON/intent short-circuit.

turns.user: Yes, I accept that.
turns.assistant: Recorded S15. Moved to S16 (seed input preservation).

turns.user: What are you talking about the recreate ability test?
turns.assistant: Conceded misapplication. Recreatability test is about whether spec is sufficient to produce downstream code, not about preserving upstream inputs. Reframed reasons: audit trail, template-quality evaluation, archaeological value. Vs reasons not to preserve: git commit can carry it, backward-looking once spec exists, invites confusion.

turns.user:
Can you just capture it as a proposed change for v000? Or a file named seed with a proper sorting filename?

Which brings up a different question. In the history folder, are all of the files named so that they will sort both chronologically and alphanumerically in the file system?
turns.assistant: Recommended not adding v000 (over-engineering). Instead: capture seed as proposed-change for v001 (history/v001/v001-proposed-change-seed.md + revision). Unifies model. On sort: honest answer - current filenames don't sort both ways cleanly. Proposal/revision pairs are adjacent but spec files straddle them. Offered three improvement options: numeric prefix, subdirectories by role, or accept current sort.

turns.user: There should be a proposed_changes subdir under each history/vnn dir. Just like the top level spec, and this preserves relative markdown links for all cross-file references. Right?
turns.assistant: Yes - and dropping vNNN- prefix from files inside history/vNNN/ becomes essential for link preservation. Walked through concrete example: active proposed-change [Foo](../spec.md#foo) resolves correctly both when active and when moved to history under parallel structure. Kept prefix would silently break. Presented full unified structure. Implications: drop 'Historic version files MUST be prefixed' rule; filename uniqueness via directory boundary; seed case unified.

turns.user: I don't like that an ide search for `spec.md` will turn up hundreds of hits. But I guess that's unaviodable while preserving relative links. But do we really care about relative links in a history folder?
turns.assistant: Re-examined tradeoff. Cases where history links matter: audit investigation, LLM reading history for evolution, recursive recreatability. Worse alternative flagged: flat history with prefix but unupdated links would SILENTLY resolve to current spec - dangerous. Three real options: (1) flat history + vNNN- prefix + rewrite links on move, (2) parallel structure + no prefix, (3) parallel + prefix + rewrite.

turns.user: option 2. Add a note that users can manually delete all their history if they don't want to keep it around. Will this violate any current doctor check?
turns.assistant: Recorded option 2. Explained yes - deleting history violates checks #4 (dir must exist), #6 (contiguity), #8 (latest+1), #9 (diff against latest). Plus seed refuses if SPECIFICATION/ exists - no recovery. Offered three handling options: disallow, make seed recover, or new rebuild-history/reset-history sub-command.

turns.user: I like the new sub-command, but this needs discussion about terminology. This is really the same process as collapsing older events in an event sourcing architecture. What would be a good name for this command?
turns.assistant: Offered terminology options from event-sourcing: collapse, compact (Kafka log compaction), snapshot, squash (git), rebase. Leaned compact as closest event-sourcing analogue.

turns.user:
squash history is perfect.

But there's one more thing I want to talk about that's worrying me on the history. You said name the original seed proposed to change v001. But this is semantically wrong. A proposed change is relative to the existing version that it applies to. But in the case of the C, there's nothing for it to apply to. That's why I said v000. But you pushed back on that, and I don't understand why.
turns.assistant: Re-examined. Original pushback was weaker than I made it. User's semantic point valid even without on-disk v000. Proposed-changes under history/vNNN/proposed_changes/ are proposals processed during revise that produced vNNN - applied to v(NNN-1). For seed: applied to v000 (implicit empty) to produce v001. Same pattern, no special case. V000 as implicit concept without on-disk directory.

turns.user:
I think there is more exploration we need to do that will clarify this.

The squash history should not reset the version to one. This will result in confusing git history where the same version of previously deleted files gets committed again.

So it can just delete the older version dirs, up to V<n-1>, which will have its contents replaced by a single `SQUASHED_HISTORY.json` file. This will record the range of extant versions which were squashed.

So it's really a `prune-history` command, and PRUNED_HISTORY.json. Change all the SQUASH terminology to PRUNE.

And the existence of PRUNED_HISTORY.json can be an indicator to doctor that it doesn't need to walk back any further, and thus all its checks should still be workable.

Is this all sensible?
turns.assistant: Yes, sensible. Verified reframed semantics: renamed squash-history -> prune-history; deletes v001 through v(N-1), preserves vN; version counter monotonic; marker file records pruned range. Walked all doctor static checks - all workable with contiguity reading the marker. Asked two follow-up decisions: marker file location and content shape.

turns.user: The pruned file lives in the oldest version dir, `v<current_version - 1>` I thought my comment made that clear.
turns.assistant: Corrected misread. Prune design: v(n-1) survives as directory with only PRUNED_HISTORY.json; everything older deleted entirely; v(n) keeps full content. Cleaner than top-of-history marker. Asked about content shape.

turns.user: 1 minimal. I'm putting a time stamp in; it doesn't make any sense. It could be performed on a git branch, so the time stamp is irrelevant if it is merged later. That's the same reason we can't put the get shad in either, because that may change if it gets re-based.
turns.assistant: Recorded minimal PRUNED_HISTORY.json: {"pruned_range": [1, 99]}. No timestamp, no SHA, no identity - all fragile under rebase/merge; git commit metadata provides audit. Consolidated all history-related decisions. S16 complete. Moved to S17.

turns.user: You are really confused. Prior art is just a directory at the top of this repo. It has nothing to do with any specification. Does the current proposal imply that anywhere, or were you just confused?
turns.assistant: Acknowledged confusion. Correct framing: prior-art/ is top-level directory in the livespec repo for attribution of the livespec project itself. Users' SPECIFICATION/ doesn't get copies. V002 acknowledgement's follow-up to 'move into SPECIFICATION/prior-art/' was wrong. Proposed dropping that follow-up.

turns.user:
No, I think there's still some confusion here. OUR version of the `livespec-nlspec-spec.md` Should still be used by the skill in some way to help enforce/encourage that proper NL spec discipline is followed.

But I'm unsure what that could look like or where it could fit in. Maybe it's out of scope, but it should just be as part of a linker for this project's specification, only for dogfooding and to see how it works for possible inclusion in the skill itself.

Thoughts?
turns.assistant: Articulated tension: current proposal positions NLSpec as skill-wide all-users discipline, but templates own content generation (including prompts for discipline). Offered three reconciled paths: (1) guidelines inside livespec template, skill auto-injects when present (matches Option 3), (2) same + also in livespec's SPECIFICATION for dogfood, (3) move NLSpec enforcement entirely out of v1. turns.user:
